walkthrough.title: Assemble the Clock
walkthrough.text: Welcome to the museum. Strange things have been happening here at night, and you are among the brave few willing to investigate. Let's get set up. First, find the cardboard clock, the clock hand, and the small plastic connector. Press the connector through the clock hand and into the center of the clock face. Set the clock hand to XII, which is midnight. Place the clock in the center of the table where everyone can see it. This clock tracks the passage of time, and every time it strikes midnight, something terrible stirs.

walkthrough.title: Place the Entrance and Dice
walkthrough.text: Now find the Entrance reference sheet. This is the card that lists activities investigators can do when they are not exploring adventures. Place it next to the clock. Then gather all the dice: six green dice, one yellow die, and one red die. Place them near the clock where everyone can reach them. The green dice are your standard investigation dice. The yellow and red dice are more powerful and are only rolled when you spend certain items.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Ancient One
walkthrough.text: This is the big bad. Find the eight Ancient One cards. These are the oversized cards depicting cosmic horrors like Yig, Azathoth, and Cthulhu. For your first game, you can pick one at random or choose as a group. Each Ancient One has a different difficulty level, doom track size, and special ability. Place the chosen Ancient One card next to the clock. The doom track on the card shows how many doom tokens it takes for the creature to awaken, and the Elder Sign limit tells you how many elder signs you need to seal it away and win.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Monster Cup
walkthrough.text: Gather all the monster markers, the small square tokens with horrifying creatures on them. Place them face down in the box lid or any opaque container you have handy. This container is called the monster cup, and you will draw from it throughout the game when monsters appear. Important: if your Ancient One is Nyarlathotep, also add the five mask monster markers to the cup. These are the markers with a mask symbol. If you chose any other Ancient One, set the mask monsters aside. They are not used.

walkthrough.title: Deal Out the Adventures
walkthrough.text: Now for the museum itself. Find the adventure deck, which consists of 48 adventure cards. Do not mix in the Other World adventure cards yet; set those eight cards aside as a separate face-down deck. Shuffle the adventure deck thoroughly and deal six cards face up in two rows of three below the clock and Entrance sheet. These six cards represent the locations in the museum that investigators can explore. If any of the dealt adventure cards have a locked die icon on them, immediately place the matching colored die on that card.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Other World Deck
walkthrough.text: Take the eight Other World adventure cards and shuffle them into their own separate face-down deck. Place this deck near the main adventure deck. Other World adventures are special, more difficult adventures that only come into play when an investigator earns a gate reward. They represent portals to other dimensions and are placed below the normal six adventure cards when they enter play.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Items, Spells, and Clues
walkthrough.text: Gather all the clue tokens and place them in a shared pool to the side of the adventure cards. Then find the four small card decks: Common Items, Unique Items, Spells, and Allies. Shuffle each deck separately and place them face down near the clue tokens. These decks are the rewards investigators can earn by completing adventures. Common Items typically let you add the yellow die to your rolls. Unique Items typically add the red die. Spells let you save a die result for later use. Allies have unique helpful abilities.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Sanity, Stamina, Doom, and Elder Sign Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place the blue sanity tokens and red stamina tokens in shared piles where everyone can reach them. These track your investigators' mental and physical health. Also set out the doom tokens and elder sign tokens nearby. Doom tokens go onto the Ancient One's doom track as the game progresses, and elder sign tokens are placed next to the Ancient One card as investigators collect them. When you have enough elder signs, you win. When the doom track fills, you are in deep trouble.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Investigators
walkthrough.text: Time to pick your heroes. There are 16 investigator cards, each with a unique name, backstory, special ability, and starting items. You can either let each player choose an investigator or deal them randomly. Each player takes their chosen investigator card and the matching investigator marker. Then collect the number of sanity tokens shown on the card as your maximum sanity, and the number of stamina tokens shown as your maximum stamina. Also take any starting items or clue tokens listed on your investigator card from the appropriate decks and token pools.

walkthrough.title: Place Investigators at the Entrance
walkthrough.text: All investigators begin the game at the museum entrance. Place every player's investigator marker on the Entrance reference sheet. Note that investigators do not have to stay here on their first turn; they can immediately move to an adventure card. The entrance is simply where everyone starts.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: Select one player to go first, either randomly or by any method your group agrees on. Play will proceed clockwise from the first player. The first player has an extra responsibility during setup: they will shuffle the Mythos deck and draw the initial Mythos card.

walkthrough.title: Draw the Initial Mythos Card
walkthrough.text: The first player shuffles the 32 Mythos cards into a face-down deck and places it next to the Ancient One card. Then they draw one Mythos card and resolve it immediately. Each Mythos card has two parts: an immediate effect on the top half, which usually adds a doom token or spawns a monster, and a lingering effect on the bottom half, which creates an ongoing rule until the next Mythos card is drawn. If this first Mythos card has a locked die icon, place the appropriate die on it. The dark powers are already at work. You are ready to play.

summary.title: Assemble the Clock
bullets: Attach clock hand to clock face using plastic connector
Set clock hand to XII (midnight)
Place clock centrally on the table

summary.title: Place the Entrance and Dice
bullets: Place Entrance reference sheet next to the clock
Place 6 green dice, 1 yellow die, and 1 red die nearby

summary.title: Choose Your Ancient One
bullets: Select 1 of 8 Ancient One cards (random or by group choice)
Place next to the clock
Note the doom track size and Elder Sign limit on the card

summary.title: Prepare the Monster Cup
bullets: Place all monster markers face down in box lid or opaque container
If Ancient One is Nyarlathotep, add 5 mask monster markers
Otherwise, set mask monsters aside — not used

summary.title: Deal Out the Adventures
bullets: Shuffle 48 adventure cards (not Other Worlds)
Deal 6 face up in 2 rows of 3 below the clock
If any show a locked die icon, place matching die on card

summary.title: Shuffle the Other World Deck
bullets: Shuffle 8 Other World adventure cards separately
Place face down near the main adventure deck
These only enter play when a gate reward is earned

summary.title: Set Up Items, Spells, and Clues
bullets: Pool all clue tokens to the side
Shuffle Common Items, Unique Items, Spells, and Allies into 4 separate decks
Common Items add yellow die; Unique Items add red die

summary.title: Set Out Sanity, Stamina, Doom, and Elder Sign Tokens
bullets: Blue sanity tokens and red stamina tokens in shared piles
Doom tokens placed on Ancient One's doom track as game progresses
Elder sign tokens placed next to Ancient One when collected

summary.title: Choose Your Investigators
bullets: Each player selects or is dealt 1 of 16 investigator cards
Take matching investigator marker
Collect starting sanity, stamina, items, and clue tokens as shown on card

summary.title: Place Investigators at the Entrance
bullets: Place all investigator markers on the Entrance reference sheet
Investigators may move away on their very first turn

summary.title: Choose the First Player
bullets: Select first player randomly or by group agreement
Play proceeds clockwise
First player handles the initial Mythos draw

summary.title: Draw the Initial Mythos Card
bullets: Shuffle 32 Mythos cards, place deck next to Ancient One
Draw and resolve 1 Mythos card immediately
Top half = immediate effect; bottom half = lingering effect
If Mythos card has locked die icon, place matching die on it

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Elder Sign is played in turns going clockwise around the table. On your turn, you do three things in order. First, you move your investigator marker to either an adventure card or the Entrance. Second, you either attempt to resolve the adventure you moved to, or you perform an Entrance activity. Third, you advance the clock by three hours. That is the whole turn. Simple on the surface, but the adventure resolution step is where the real tension lives.

walkthrough.title: Step 1: Move Your Investigator
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, pick up your investigator marker and place it on one of the six face-up adventure cards or on the Entrance reference sheet. You can go anywhere, even to the same card you were on last turn, and even to a card that another investigator is already on. If another investigator is on the same adventure card, they can assist you during your dice rolls. Moving to the Entrance means you skip the adventure and instead use one of the Entrance's three services.

walkthrough.title: Step 2a: Attempting an Adventure — Rolling Dice
walkthrough.text: If you moved to an adventure card, you now attempt to resolve it. You start by rolling all six green dice. If any game effect has given you the yellow die, the red die, or both, you roll those too. However, if a die is locked on a card or marker elsewhere, you cannot roll it. Look at each adventure card. It shows one or more horizontal rows of symbols. Each row is a task. You must complete all tasks on the card to successfully resolve the adventure.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Tasks
walkthrough.text: Each task is a row of symbols representing die results you need. Investigation symbols show a number, and you need dice showing investigation results that add up to at least that number. Lore, peril, and terror symbols each require a specific matching die face. Some tasks show a split symbol, which means either of the two results shown will satisfy it. Some tasks also require you to advance the clock, lose sanity, or lose stamina as part of completing them. You may only complete one task per dice roll, even if you have results that could satisfy multiple tasks.

walkthrough.title: Completing a Task
walkthrough.text: When you roll the dice and can match all requirements of a single task, you complete that task. Place the matching dice on top of the corresponding symbols. Those dice are now spent and cannot be rolled again this turn. If the task requires you to lose sanity, stamina, or advance the clock, do that now. Important: you cannot complete a task if it would reduce your sanity or stamina to zero or below. After completing a task, if more tasks remain on the card, take all the unspent dice and roll them again to attempt the next task.

walkthrough.title: Failing to Complete a Task
walkthrough.text: If you roll the dice and cannot or choose not to complete any task, you have failed this roll. First, if any of your dice show a terror result, resolve all terror effects on the adventure card and on the current Mythos card. Then you have a choice: try again or give up entirely. If you try again, you must set one die aside permanently — it is out for this attempt. You may optionally focus one additional die by placing it on your investigator marker, saving its result for later use. Then re-roll all remaining dice. If you give up, you fail the adventure.

walkthrough.title: Focusing and Assisting
walkthrough.text: Focusing is a lifeline. When you fail a roll, after setting one die aside, you may place one other die on your own investigator marker without changing its result. That die will not be re-rolled, but its result can still be used to satisfy a task requirement on a future roll. This is focusing. If another investigator's marker is on the same adventure card, you can instead place the die on their marker. This is assisting. Each marker can hold only one die at a time. If you ultimately fail the adventure, any assisting investigators must lose one sanity or one stamina.

walkthrough.title: Using Items, Spells, and Clues
walkthrough.text: Before any roll during your adventure attempt, you can spend items and spells from your hand. Most Common Items add the yellow die to your pool. Most Unique Items add the red die. The yellow die is like a green die but replaces the terror face with a four-investigation face. The red die replaces the one-investigation face with a wildcard that counts as any result. Spells let you place a die on the spell card after rolling, saving its result for future rolls. After any roll, you can spend clue tokens to re-roll any or all dice in your pool. You can spend multiple clues on the same roll.

walkthrough.title: Resolving an Adventure — Rewards and Penalties
walkthrough.text: When you complete all tasks on an adventure card, you receive the rewards shown in the green area at the bottom of the card. Rewards may include items, spells, allies, clue tokens, and the coveted elder sign tokens. You then move all investigators on that card back to the Entrance, take the adventure card as a trophy worth a certain number of trophies, and draw a new adventure card to replace it. When you fail an adventure, you suffer the penalties shown in the red area, which usually involve losing sanity, stamina, or items. Then remove your unspent dice from the card.

walkthrough.title: Step 2b: Waiting at the Entrance
walkthrough.text: If you moved to the Entrance instead of an adventure, you choose one of three services. Receive First Aid: regain one sanity and one stamina for free, or pay two trophies to fully restore one of them, or four trophies to fully restore both. Search the Lost and Found: roll a green die and consult the chart on the Entrance sheet for a random reward. Buy a Souvenir: spend trophies to purchase a Common Item, Unique Item, Spell, or other benefits listed on the Entrance sheet.

walkthrough.title: Step 3: Advance the Clock
walkthrough.text: After resolving your adventure or Entrance activity, you advance the clock hand three hours clockwise. It moves from XII to III, from III to VI, from VI to IX, or from IX back to XII. The clock only ever rests on these four positions. If the clock reaches XII, midnight strikes. Resolve all At Midnight effects currently in play, then draw a new Mythos card and resolve its immediate effect. The lingering effect replaces the previous Mythos card's lingering effect. Your turn is now over. Play passes to the left.

walkthrough.title: Monsters
walkthrough.text: When a game effect says a monster appears, draw a random monster marker from the monster cup and place it on a monster task on an adventure card. Monster tasks are outlined with a white border. An empty monster task gets completely replaced by the monster marker. A total monster task also gets replaced. A partial monster task adds the monster's requirements to the remaining uncovered requirements. If no monster tasks are available, add the monster as an extra task under an adventure card. When you complete a monster's task, you take the marker and gain any reward on its back.

walkthrough.title: The Doom Track and Being Devoured
walkthrough.text: Throughout the game, doom tokens accumulate on the Ancient One's doom track. Mythos cards, terror effects, and other events add doom. When a doom token lands on a space with a monster icon, a monster also appears. If an investigator's sanity or stamina drops to zero, they are devoured. Add one doom token to the doom track, return the investigator and all their belongings to the box and decks. That player gets a new investigator on their next turn, starting fresh. If no investigators remain, that player is eliminated but can still win if others succeed.

walkthrough.title: Winning, Losing, and the Final Battle
walkthrough.text: You win by collecting elder sign tokens equal to or greater than the number shown on the Ancient One's Elder Sign limit. You can win at any time this threshold is met. If the doom track fills completely, the Ancient One awakens and all investigators are moved to its card for a desperate final battle. Turns change: instead of adventures, each player rolls dice to complete the Ancient One's combat task, removing one doom token per success. Instead of Mythos cards at midnight, the Ancient One attacks. If all investigators are devoured during this battle, you lose. If you remove all doom tokens or reach the Elder Sign limit, you still win.

summary.title: Turn Structure Overview
bullets: Each turn: Move, Resolve Adventure (or Entrance), Advance Clock
Play proceeds clockwise
All tension centers on the dice-based adventure resolution

summary.title: Step 1: Move Your Investigator
bullets: Place marker on any face-up adventure card or the Entrance
Multiple investigators can be on the same card
Co-location enables the assist mechanic

summary.title: Step 2a: Attempting an Adventure — Rolling Dice
bullets: Roll 6 green dice (plus yellow/red if items were spent)
Locked dice cannot be rolled
Compare results to task requirements on the adventure card

summary.title: Understanding Tasks
bullets: Each row of symbols on an adventure card is one task
Investigation (number), lore, peril, terror — match die faces
Split symbols accept either result shown
Only 1 task may be completed per roll

summary.title: Completing a Task
bullets: Place matching dice on the task's symbols — those dice are spent
Pay any sanity/stamina/clock costs at that time
Cannot complete a task that would reduce sanity or stamina to 0
Re-roll remaining dice to attempt the next task

summary.title: Failing to Complete a Task
bullets: If any dice show terror, resolve all terror effects
To retry: set 1 die aside permanently, then re-roll remaining dice
May also focus or assist with 1 additional die
To give up: fail the adventure and suffer penalties

summary.title: Focusing and Assisting
bullets: Focus: save 1 die on your own marker (not re-rolled, but usable)
Assist: save 1 die on another investigator's marker on the same card
Each marker holds at most 1 die
If adventure fails, assisting investigators lose 1 sanity or 1 stamina

summary.title: Using Items, Spells, and Clues
bullets: Common Items add yellow die; Unique Items add red die
Yellow die: 4-investigation instead of terror face
Red die: wildcard face (counts as any result)
Spells save a die result for future rolls; clue tokens allow re-rolls

summary.title: Resolving an Adventure — Rewards and Penalties
bullets: Success: receive rewards (items, spells, allies, clues, elder signs)
Take the card as a trophy; draw a replacement adventure
Failure: suffer penalties (lose sanity, stamina, items)
All investigators on the card return to the Entrance on success

summary.title: Step 2b: Waiting at the Entrance
bullets: Receive First Aid: restore 1 sanity + 1 stamina free, or pay trophies for full restore
Search the Lost & Found: roll a green die, consult chart for reward
Buy a Souvenir: spend trophies for items from the Entrance list

summary.title: Step 3: Advance the Clock
bullets: Move clock hand 3 hours clockwise (III, VI, IX, XII)
At midnight: resolve At Midnight effects, then draw and resolve a new Mythos card
Mythos immediate effect happens now; lingering effect persists until next Mythos

summary.title: Monsters
bullets: Draw from monster cup, place on a monster task (white-bordered)
Empty/total tasks are replaced; partial tasks are augmented
No open monster tasks? Add monster as extra task under any adventure
Completing a monster's task earns its trophy and back-side reward

summary.title: The Doom Track and Being Devoured
bullets: Doom tokens fill the doom track via Mythos, terror effects, and devouring
Doom spaces with monster icons also spawn a monster
Investigator at 0 sanity or 0 stamina is devoured — adds 1 doom token
Devoured player gets a new investigator next turn (if any remain)

summary.title: Winning, Losing, and the Final Battle
bullets: Win: collect elder signs >= Ancient One's Elder Sign limit
Doom track full: Ancient One awakens, final battle begins
Battle turns: roll dice to complete combat task, remove doom tokens
Midnight during battle: Ancient One attacks instead of Mythos draw
All devoured = lose; remove all doom or reach elder sign limit = win

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Museum
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with three investigators — let's call them the Professor, the Psychic, and the Drifter. We will follow their first few turns exploring the museum, rolling dice, and confronting the supernatural. Even if you have more or fewer players, follow along to see the core mechanics in action. The Ancient One for this tutorial is Yig, Father of Serpents, who has a manageable doom track and a straightforward combat task.

walkthrough.title: The Museum Is Open
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. Six adventure cards are face up on the table. The clock reads XII. The first Mythos card has been drawn and resolved, likely adding a doom token or spawning a monster. All three investigators are at the Entrance. Each has their starting sanity, stamina, items, and clue tokens based on their investigator cards. The Professor starts with a Spell. The Psychic starts with a Clue token. The Drifter starts with a Common Item. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: The Professor's First Turn: Choosing an Adventure
walkthrough.text: The Professor goes first. Look at the six face-up adventure cards. Each card shows its tasks, any special effects, and its rewards and penalties. The Professor picks an adventure with two tasks and a modest reward that includes a clue token. The tasks require investigation results totaling 6 and a lore result. This looks achievable with six green dice. The Professor moves their marker to that card.

walkthrough.title: Rolling the Dice
walkthrough.text: The Professor rolls all six green dice. The results are: 3 investigation, 2 investigation, 1 investigation, lore, peril, and terror. Good news: the first task requires investigation results totaling at least 3, and the Professor has a 3. But wait — tasks require you to match ALL symbols in a single task row with one roll. Let's say the first task requires just a 3-investigation. The Professor places the die showing 3 on that task. Task one complete. Now the remaining five dice are re-rolled for task two.

walkthrough.title: Completing the Adventure
walkthrough.text: The Professor re-rolls the five remaining dice and gets: 2 investigation, 1 investigation, lore, peril, terror. Task two requires a lore result. The Professor has it. They place the lore die on the task. Both tasks are done. The Professor has resolved the adventure. They collect the rewards: a clue token from the supply. The adventure card is taken as a trophy. All investigators on the card (just the Professor) move to the Entrance. A new adventure card is dealt face up to replace the resolved one.

walkthrough.title: Advancing the Clock
walkthrough.text: The Professor advances the clock from XII to III. No midnight this time, so no Mythos card is drawn. The Professor's turn is over. Play passes to the Psychic.

walkthrough.title: The Psychic's Turn: A Harder Adventure
walkthrough.text: The Psychic chooses an adventure with three tasks and an elder sign as one of the rewards. This is tempting but risky. More tasks means more dice rolls needed, and you lose dice along the way as you complete tasks and set dice aside on failures. The Psychic moves to this card and rolls all six green dice.

walkthrough.title: The Psychic Fails a Roll
walkthrough.text: The Psychic completes the first task, using two dice. Four dice remain. They re-roll and get results that do not match any remaining task. This is a failed roll. One of the dice shows terror, so the Psychic must resolve all terror effects on the adventure card and the current Mythos card. The terror effect on this card says: lose 1 sanity. The Psychic loses a sanity token.

walkthrough.title: The Psychic Focuses a Die
walkthrough.text: The Psychic wants to try again. They must set one die aside permanently. They choose the die with the worst result and remove it. Now they have three dice. Before re-rolling, the Psychic decides to focus. They take one of the remaining dice, a lore result, and place it on their own investigator marker. This die will not be re-rolled, but its lore result can still be used to satisfy a lore requirement on a future roll. Now the Psychic re-rolls the remaining two dice.

walkthrough.title: Using a Clue Token
walkthrough.text: The Psychic re-rolls two dice and gets 1 investigation and peril. The second task needs a lore and a 2-investigation. The Psychic has the lore saved on their marker, but only 1 investigation showing. Time to use that starting clue token. The Psychic spends one clue, returning it to the pool, and re-rolls the die showing 1 investigation. It comes up 2 investigation. Combined with the focused lore die, the Psychic can now complete task two. Clue tokens are powerful. Never forget you have them.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake 1: Forgetting to Focus
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake new players make constantly. When you fail a roll, you MUST set one die aside, but many players forget that you MAY also focus another die. Focusing saves a useful result for later. In the Psychic's case, focusing that lore die was the key to completing task two. If the Psychic had simply set a die aside and re-rolled everything else, they would have needed to roll both lore AND 2-investigation on just two dice. Focusing dramatically improves your odds. Always consider it when you fail a roll.

walkthrough.title: The Psychic Finishes and Earns an Elder Sign
walkthrough.text: With task two complete, the Psychic has one die left. They roll it for task three, which only requires a peril result. The die shows... terror. Not peril. But the Psychic has no more clues to re-roll with. They set the die aside and re-roll zero dice. Wait, they cannot roll zero dice. The Psychic automatically fails to resolve the adventure. They suffer the penalties: lose 1 stamina. Actually, let us rewind. The Psychic still had one more die from the assist they could have used. Let us say the Psychic completes all three tasks over the course of their rolls. They earn the elder sign reward. One elder sign token is placed next to the Ancient One card. Progress.

walkthrough.title: The Drifter's Turn: Using an Item
walkthrough.text: The Drifter picks an adventure and rolls the six green dice. Before rolling, though, the Drifter decides to spend their starting Common Item. This adds the yellow die to the pool, so the Drifter rolls seven dice total. The yellow die is identical to a green die except it has a 4-investigation face instead of terror. This makes it strictly better than a green die. The Drifter rolls well and completes the first task.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake 2: Misunderstanding Task Completion Order
walkthrough.text: The Drifter sees two remaining tasks. One looks easy and one looks hard. The Drifter wants to do the easy one first to save more dice for the hard one. But wait — does this adventure card have an arrow on its left side? Check carefully. If there is an arrow, you MUST complete tasks from top to bottom in order. If there is no arrow, you can complete them in any order. Many new players either miss the arrow and complete tasks out of order, or they assume all tasks must go top to bottom when they actually have free choice. Always check for the arrow.

walkthrough.title: The Drifter Completes Their Adventure
walkthrough.text: The adventure has no arrow, so the Drifter completes the easier task first, preserving more dice for the harder one. Smart play. They succeed, collect their rewards, take the card as a trophy, and a new adventure replaces it. The Drifter advances the clock from VI to IX. No midnight yet.

walkthrough.title: Midnight Strikes
walkthrough.text: The Professor starts their second turn, chooses an adventure, and resolves it. Then they advance the clock from IX to XII. Midnight. First, resolve any At Midnight effects from the lingering portion of the current Mythos card. Perhaps all investigators lose 1 stamina, or a monster appears. Then the Professor draws a new Mythos card. The immediate effect fires: add 1 doom token to the doom track. The doom track creeps closer to full. The new lingering effect takes hold, replacing the old one.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake 3: Doom Track Errors
walkthrough.text: A very common mistake involves the doom track. Players sometimes forget to add doom tokens when an investigator is devoured. Being devoured adds 1 doom token in addition to losing the investigator. Players also forget that certain doom track spaces have monster icons on them. When a doom token lands on one of those spaces, a monster immediately appears from the monster cup. Always check the doom track space when placing a token. Missing a monster spawn can make the game feel easier than it should be.

walkthrough.title: The Psychic Visits the Entrance
walkthrough.text: The Psychic is hurting. They lost sanity earlier and are getting low. Instead of attempting a risky adventure, the Psychic moves to the Entrance and chooses Receive First Aid. They regain 1 sanity and 1 stamina for free. It is not glamorous, but staying alive matters more than glory. A devoured investigator adds a doom token, wasting precious time. Sometimes the smartest play is to heal.

walkthrough.title: The Drifter Attempts a Monster Adventure
walkthrough.text: One of the adventure cards now has a monster marker on it, placed there by the last Mythos card. The Drifter decides to tackle this card. The monster has replaced one of the card's tasks with its own requirements. The Drifter will need to complete the monster's task along with the card's remaining tasks. If the Drifter succeeds, they get both the adventure card reward and the monster marker's reward on its back. Risky but efficient.

walkthrough.title: Cooperation in Action
walkthrough.text: The Professor decides to move to the same adventure card as the Drifter on their next turn. Even though the Professor does not attempt the adventure themselves — it is not their turn — their presence on the card means the Drifter can use the assist mechanic. If the Drifter fails a roll, they can place a focused die on the Professor's investigator marker. This effectively saves an extra die result. But beware: if the Drifter ultimately fails the adventure, the Professor must lose 1 sanity or 1 stamina as a consequence of assisting.

walkthrough.title: Trophies and the Entrance Shop
walkthrough.text: After a few turns, the Drifter has collected several adventure cards and a monster marker as trophies. Each trophy has a value printed on it. Adventure cards are usually worth 2 trophies, and monster markers are worth 1. The Drifter can spend these trophies at the Entrance to buy items, heal fully, or acquire other benefits. Managing your trophy economy matters, especially in longer games where items and healing become critical.

walkthrough.title: The Game Takes Shape
walkthrough.text: After several rounds of play, the pattern becomes clear. Each turn is a calculated risk: pick an adventure you think you can handle, roll the dice, use your items and clues wisely, and pray the clock does not bring something terrible at midnight. Elder signs accumulate slowly. Doom tokens accumulate steadily. The race is on. Your group needs to coordinate which adventures to tackle, when to heal, and when to spend precious resources.

walkthrough.title: Other Worlds
walkthrough.text: One of the rewards on an adventure card shows a gate icon. When earned, draw an Other World adventure card and place it below the normal six adventures. Other World cards are harder but offer better rewards, often including elder signs. They are not replaced when resolved, so they are one-time opportunities. Any investigator can move to an Other World card on their turn just like a normal adventure.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen the complete flow of Elder Sign: choosing adventures, rolling and re-rolling dice, focusing and assisting, using items and clues, dealing with midnight and Mythos cards, fighting monsters, tracking doom, and earning elder signs. You have also seen the three most common mistakes: forgetting to focus dice, misunderstanding task completion order, and doom track errors. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Investigate
walkthrough.text:
Before you face the Ancient One, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and priority guidance.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to dice faces, tokens, card types, and Lovecraftian terminology.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. The museum awaits.

summary.title: Welcome to the Museum
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 investigators: Professor, Psychic, Drifter
Using Yig as the Ancient One for a manageable first game
Follow along regardless of your actual player count

summary.title: The Museum Is Open
bullets: 6 adventure cards face up, clock at XII, initial Mythos resolved
All investigators start at the Entrance with their starting gear
Professor has a Spell, Psychic has a Clue, Drifter has a Common Item

summary.title: The Professor's First Turn: Choosing an Adventure
bullets: Evaluate adventure cards by task difficulty and rewards
Pick an adventure with manageable tasks for your first attempt
Move investigator marker to the chosen card

summary.title: Rolling the Dice
bullets: Roll all 6 green dice to start
Compare results to the task requirements on the card
Complete 1 task at a time — place matching dice on the task symbols

summary.title: Completing the Adventure
bullets: Complete all tasks to resolve the adventure
Collect rewards, take card as trophy, new card replaces it
All investigators on the card return to Entrance

summary.title: Advancing the Clock
bullets: Move clock 3 hours (XII to III, III to VI, etc.)
No midnight = no Mythos card drawn
Turn over, play passes clockwise

summary.title: The Psychic's Turn: A Harder Adventure
bullets: More tasks = more risk but better rewards (like elder signs)
Each completed task removes dice from your pool
Harder adventures require careful resource management

summary.title: The Psychic Fails a Roll
bullets: Failed roll with terror showing = resolve terror effects
Terror effects on adventure card AND current Mythos card apply
Common terror penalty: lose 1 sanity

summary.title: The Psychic Focuses a Die
bullets: After failing: must set 1 die aside permanently
May also focus 1 die on your own investigator marker
Focused die keeps its result and can satisfy future task requirements

summary.title: Using a Clue Token
bullets: Spend a clue token after any roll to re-roll any/all dice
Can spend multiple clues on the same roll
Spent clues return to the shared pool

summary.title: Common Mistake 1: Forgetting to Focus
bullets: Focusing is OPTIONAL — many new players forget it exists
Saving a useful die result dramatically improves your odds
Always consider focusing when you fail a roll

summary.title: The Psychic Finishes and Earns an Elder Sign
bullets: Completing all tasks on a hard adventure earns premium rewards
Elder sign tokens are placed next to the Ancient One card
Each elder sign brings you closer to sealing the Ancient One

summary.title: The Drifter's Turn: Using an Item
bullets: Spend a Common Item before rolling to add the yellow die (7 dice total)
Yellow die has 4-investigation instead of terror — strictly better
Items are spent to the bottom of their deck after use

summary.title: Common Mistake 2: Misunderstanding Task Completion Order
bullets: Arrow on left side of card = tasks must go top to bottom
No arrow = complete tasks in any order you choose
Always check for the arrow before planning your approach

summary.title: The Drifter Completes Their Adventure
bullets: No arrow = choose easier tasks first to preserve dice
Collect rewards, take trophy card, new adventure replaces it
Advance clock 3 hours

summary.title: Midnight Strikes
bullets: Clock reaches XII: resolve At Midnight lingering effects first
Then draw and resolve a new Mythos card
Immediate effect fires now; lingering effect replaces previous one

summary.title: Common Mistake 3: Doom Track Errors
bullets: Devoured investigators add 1 doom token — don't forget
Some doom track spaces have monster icons — spawn a monster when filled
Always check the doom space when placing a token

summary.title: The Psychic Visits the Entrance
bullets: Low on health? Visit the Entrance for First Aid
Regain 1 sanity + 1 stamina for free, or pay trophies for full restore
Staying alive prevents doom token from devouring

summary.title: The Drifter Attempts a Monster Adventure
bullets: Monsters add or replace tasks on adventure cards
Complete monster task + adventure tasks for both rewards
Monster marker rewards are printed on its back

summary.title: Cooperation in Action
bullets: Move to same card as another investigator to enable assisting
Assisting lets the active player save a die on your marker
Risk: if adventure fails, assisting investigators lose 1 sanity or stamina

summary.title: Trophies and the Entrance Shop
bullets: Adventure cards = usually 2 trophies; monster markers = 1 trophy
Spend trophies at the Entrance for items, healing, or souvenirs
Managing trophy economy is key for longer games

summary.title: The Game Takes Shape
bullets: Elder signs accumulate slowly; doom tokens accumulate steadily
Coordinate who tackles which adventures and when to heal
Every turn is a calculated risk versus reward decision

summary.title: Other Worlds
bullets: Gate rewards open Other World adventure cards
Harder than normal adventures but offer better rewards
Not replaced when resolved — one-time opportunities

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You've seen the full flow: adventures, dice, items, midnight, monsters
Three common mistakes covered: forgetting focus, task order, doom errors
Ready for a full game against any Ancient One

summary.title: Before You Investigate
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy and priority guidance
Reference the Appendix for dice faces, tokens, and terminology
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime via the Q&A tab

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before strategy, let's cover the traps that catch new investigators. First, forgetting to focus dice when you fail a roll. Focusing saves a useful result and dramatically improves your odds on the retry. Second, spending all your clue tokens early. Clues are your safety net; save at least one for a critical adventure. Third, ignoring the Entrance. Healing is not exciting, but a devoured investigator adds a doom token and wastes turns. Fourth, tackling adventures that are too difficult with too few dice. Count the total requirements before committing. Fifth, forgetting that some adventure cards require tasks to be completed in order, from top to bottom, when there is an arrow.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Adventures with Elder Signs
walkthrough.text: Elder signs are how you win. Every adventure you complete that does not award an elder sign is buying time at best. Scan the face-up adventures and prioritize any that offer an elder sign reward, even if they are harder. That said, do not throw away investigators on impossible adventures. If the elder sign adventure has four difficult tasks and you have no items, consider tackling an easier adventure first to build up your resources, then go for the elder sign next turn.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Health
walkthrough.text: Sanity and stamina are your life. When either hits zero, your investigator is devoured, which adds a doom token and costs you all your items, trophies, and progress. Monitor both values constantly. If either drops to two or below, seriously consider visiting the Entrance for first aid. The free option gives you back one sanity and one stamina, which is often enough to stay in the fight. It is almost always better to spend one turn healing than to lose an investigator.

walkthrough.title: Use Items at the Right Time
walkthrough.text: Common Items and Unique Items add the yellow or red die to your pool. The yellow die replaces the terror face with 4-investigation, making it strictly better than a green die. The red die goes further, replacing 1-investigation with a wildcard that can be any result. Use items on adventures that really matter, especially ones with elder sign rewards or ones that would be devastating to fail. Do not waste a Unique Item on an easy two-task adventure. Save it for the three-task elder sign adventure that could seal the game.

walkthrough.title: Coordinate with Your Team
walkthrough.text: Elder Sign is cooperative, so talk strategy openly. Discuss which adventures to attempt, who should heal, and when to use items. The assist mechanic is powerful but risky. Having a second investigator on the same card gives the active player an extra focused die, but if the adventure fails, the assisting investigator suffers. Coordinate your positions. A good plan: have a high-sanity investigator assist on dangerous adventures so they can absorb the penalty if things go wrong.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Clock
walkthrough.text: The clock advances three hours every turn. With four players, midnight comes once per round. With fewer players, midnight comes more frequently per player. This means in a two-player game, you are drawing Mythos cards more often relative to your actions. Plan around the clock. If the clock is at IX and it is your turn, midnight will strike when you advance. Prepare for the Mythos card. Make sure you have buffer health, and consider that a lingering At Midnight effect might punish everyone.

walkthrough.title: Trophy Management
walkthrough.text: Resolved adventure cards and defeated monster markers become trophies you can spend at the Entrance. Adventures are usually worth two trophies, monsters worth one. The Entrance shop offers items, healing, and other benefits. Do not hoard trophies forever, but do not spend them frivolously either. A good guideline: spend trophies when you desperately need healing or when a specific shop item would make a critical upcoming adventure achievable. Keep at least two trophies in reserve for emergencies.

walkthrough.title: Respect the Doom Track
walkthrough.text: The doom track fills from Mythos cards, terror effects, devouring, and special Ancient One abilities. Once it fills, the Ancient One awakens and the final battle begins. The final battle is extremely difficult. Treat it as a near-certain loss unless you are very close to the elder sign limit. Your best strategy is to prevent the awakening entirely by earning elder signs quickly and avoiding unnecessary doom tokens. Keep investigators alive, deal with monsters before they pile up, and rush elder sign adventures when the doom track is getting dangerously full.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Forgetting to focus dice after a failed roll
Spending all clue tokens too early — save at least 1 for emergencies
Ignoring the Entrance — healing prevents doom-adding devouring
Tackling too-hard adventures with insufficient dice
Missing the arrow icon that forces top-to-bottom task order

summary.title: Prioritize Adventures with Elder Signs
bullets: Elder signs are the win condition — prioritize adventures that award them
Build up resources on easier adventures first if needed
Don't throw investigators at impossible elder sign adventures unprepared

summary.title: Manage Your Health
bullets: Monitor both sanity and stamina constantly
At 2 or below in either, consider visiting Entrance for first aid
Devouring costs 1 doom token + all items/trophies — avoid at all costs
Free first aid: +1 sanity and +1 stamina (often enough)

summary.title: Use Items at the Right Time
bullets: Yellow die (Common Item): 4-investigation instead of terror
Red die (Unique Item): wildcard face = any result
Save powerful items for elder sign adventures or high-stakes attempts
Don't waste a Unique Item on a trivial two-task adventure

summary.title: Coordinate with Your Team
bullets: Discuss strategy openly — it's cooperative
Assist mechanic: second investigator on same card gives extra focused die
Risk: if adventure fails, assisting investigator loses 1 sanity or stamina
High-sanity investigators are ideal assistants for dangerous adventures

summary.title: Watch the Clock
bullets: Clock advances 3 hours per turn; midnight triggers Mythos
Fewer players = more frequent midnight per investigator
If clock is at IX, midnight comes on your advance — prepare
At Midnight lingering effects can punish the whole group

summary.title: Trophy Management
bullets: Adventure cards = 2 trophies; monster markers = 1 trophy
Spend at Entrance for items, healing, or souvenirs
Keep at least 2 trophies in reserve for emergencies
Don't hoard forever — items from the shop can be game-changing

summary.title: Respect the Doom Track
bullets: Doom fills via Mythos, terror effects, devouring, Ancient One abilities
Final battle is near-certain loss — prevent awakening if possible
Rush elder sign adventures when doom gets dangerously high
Keep investigators alive to avoid devouring doom penalties

walkthrough.title: Investigator Synergies
walkthrough.text: Not all investigators are created equal, and certain combinations work better together. Investigators with high sanity pair well with those who have high stamina, providing balanced coverage. Investigators whose special abilities grant bonus dice or free clue tokens should lead the charge on elder sign adventures. Those with healing abilities or starting spells can play a support role. Before the game starts, look at everyone's investigator cards together and discuss who should take the lead on dangerous adventures and who should provide backup through the assist mechanic.

walkthrough.title: Adventure Prioritization
walkthrough.text: Not every adventure card is worth your time. Learn to evaluate them quickly. First, count the total number of dice results required across all tasks. Then consider how many of those require specific results like lore, peril, or terror. Investigation results are the most flexible because multiple dice can combine to reach a target. Adventures requiring mostly investigation results are statistically easier. An adventure with three tasks all requiring terror or peril is a nightmare. Factor in the reward: an elder sign is always worth extra risk, while a single common item might not be worth a difficult three-task card.

walkthrough.title: Resource Economy and Item Timing
walkthrough.text: Advanced play is about resource management. You start with limited items and clues, and the only ways to get more are through adventure rewards, the Entrance shop, or certain investigator abilities. Think of items as investment: a Common Item adds the yellow die, which increases your expected successes per roll by roughly 15 percent. A Unique Item with the red die and its wildcard face can be the difference between success and failure on a critical task. Spend items on adventures where the reward justifies the cost. An elder sign adventure is almost always worth an item. A monster-heavy card with mediocre rewards is not.

walkthrough.title: Clue Token Strategy
walkthrough.text: Clue tokens are the most flexible resource in Elder Sign. They let you re-roll any or all dice after any roll, and you can spend multiple clues on the same roll. Advanced players treat clues as insurance policies. Save clues for moments when you have already completed two of three tasks and just need one more result. The expected value of a clue re-roll is highest when you are re-rolling only one or two dice targeting a specific result. Never spend a clue to re-roll all six dice on the first roll of a turn; the odds of getting what you need are roughly the same as your original roll.

walkthrough.title: Spell Mastery
walkthrough.text: Spells are underrated by new players. A spell lets you lock a die result after rolling, saving it for a future roll on the same adventure. This is different from focusing because the die on a spell card is available regardless of whether you failed or succeeded on the previous roll. Use spells to save rare results like lore that you know you will need for a later task. The die stays on the spell card until you choose to use it. A well-timed spell effectively gives you a guaranteed result on a future roll, turning a three-task adventure from a gamble into a manageable challenge.

walkthrough.title: Managing the Midnight Cycle
walkthrough.text: With experience, you can anticipate and plan around midnight. Count the remaining turns before midnight based on the current clock position and number of players. If midnight will strike on your turn, consider whether you want to spend your turn on a risky adventure or play it safe at the Entrance. After midnight, assess the new Mythos card's lingering effect and adapt your plans. Some lingering effects are devastating; others are mild. If the current lingering effect says investigators lose 2 stamina at midnight, your team should prioritize healing before the next midnight cycle.

walkthrough.title: Monster Management
walkthrough.text: Monsters make adventures harder by adding or replacing tasks. Left unchecked, monsters accumulate and can make the entire museum nearly impossible to navigate. Advanced players monitor the monster situation and clear them proactively. A monster on an easy adventure card can be dealt with efficiently by a well-equipped investigator, earning both the adventure reward and the monster's back-side reward. Monsters on hard adventures should sometimes be avoided in favor of other cards. Remember: if no monster tasks are open, new monsters add extra tasks to existing cards, creating ever-harder adventures. Keep the museum clean.

walkthrough.title: The Final Battle — If It Comes to That
walkthrough.text: If the doom track fills, the Ancient One awakens. During the final battle, each player rolls dice on their turn to complete the Ancient One's combat task. Each success removes one doom token. At midnight, the Ancient One attacks instead of a Mythos draw, usually reducing maximum sanity or stamina and adding more doom. The key to surviving the final battle is arriving with as many items, spells, and clue tokens as possible. If you sense the doom track is about to fill, stockpile resources rather than spending them on adventures. Focus and assist do not work during the battle, so items and clues are your only lifelines.

walkthrough.title: Ancient One Matchups
walkthrough.text: Each Ancient One plays differently. Yig is the easiest, with a small doom track and simple combat task. Azathoth has no final battle — if the doom track fills, you simply lose. This makes him terrifyingly unforgiving but also straightforward: rush elder signs as fast as possible. Cthulhu has a large doom track but devastating attacks. Hastur and Nyarlathotep introduce unique mechanics like mask monsters. Before the game, read your Ancient One's special ability and attack carefully. Some abilities change fundamental rules, like preventing investigators from receiving certain rewards or adding doom tokens under specific conditions. Adjust your strategy accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Acceleration
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, you should feel increasing urgency. If you have collected half the required elder signs by the time the doom track is half full, you are on pace. If you are behind, it is time to take bigger risks. Put your best-equipped investigator on the hardest elder sign adventure. Have teammates assist. Spend items and clues liberally. In the endgame, resources are worthless if you do not win. A clue token saved is a clue token wasted if the Ancient One awakens. Conversely, if you are ahead on elder signs and the doom track is only half full, play conservatively. Do not lose investigators to unnecessary risks. Seal the Ancient One methodically.

summary.title: Investigator Synergies
bullets: Pair high-sanity and high-stamina investigators for coverage
Investigators with bonus dice/clues should lead elder sign attempts
Support investigators can heal or assist
Discuss roles before the game starts

summary.title: Adventure Prioritization
bullets: Count total required dice results — fewer = easier
Investigation results are most flexible (dice combine to hit target)
Adventures requiring lore/peril/terror are statistically harder
Elder sign reward = worth extra risk; mediocre reward = skip if hard

summary.title: Resource Economy and Item Timing
bullets: Common Items: ~15% boost via yellow die
Unique Items: red die wildcard can clinch critical tasks
Spend items where reward justifies cost (elder sign adventures)
Don't burn items on low-reward, monster-heavy cards

summary.title: Clue Token Strategy
bullets: Clues are insurance — save for critical moments, not first rolls
Best value: re-rolling 1-2 dice for a specific result
Can spend multiple clues on one roll for compound re-rolling
Re-rolling all 6 dice on turn 1 is rarely better than the original roll

summary.title: Spell Mastery
bullets: Spells lock a die result after rolling, available for any future roll
Different from focusing: not dependent on failing a roll
Save rare results (lore, peril) you know a later task needs
Turns a 3-task gamble into a manageable challenge

summary.title: Managing the Midnight Cycle
bullets: Count turns until midnight based on clock and player count
Plan risky adventures for non-midnight turns when possible
Assess each new Mythos lingering effect and adapt
Prioritize healing before devastating At Midnight effects

summary.title: Monster Management
bullets: Monsters accumulate and make adventures progressively harder
Clear monsters on easy cards — efficient twofer rewards
Avoid monsters on already-difficult cards if possible
No open monster tasks = new monsters add extra tasks to cards

summary.title: The Final Battle — If It Comes to That
bullets: Complete Ancient One's combat task to remove doom tokens
Focus and assist do NOT work during the final battle
Stockpile items and clues before doom track fills
Ancient One attacks at midnight instead of Mythos card

summary.title: Ancient One Matchups
bullets: Yig: easiest, small doom track, simple combat
Azathoth: no final battle — doom full = instant loss
Cthulhu: large doom track but devastating attacks
Nyarlathotep: adds mask monsters for unique challenges
Read your Ancient One's abilities before planning strategy

summary.title: Endgame Acceleration
bullets: Half elder signs by half doom = on pace
Behind? Take bigger risks, spend resources freely, assist on elder sign adventures
Ahead? Play conservatively, avoid unnecessary investigator losses
In endgame, saved resources are wasted if you don't win

entries.definition: A die face showing a magnifying glass with a number (1, 2, 3, or 4). Used to satisfy investigation requirements on tasks. Multiple investigation dice can be combined to meet a single investigation requirement. Green dice have 1, 2, and 3; the yellow die also has 4; the red die replaces 1 with the wildcard.
A die face showing a scroll symbol. Required by certain tasks. Lore appears on one face of each green die, one face of the yellow die, and one face of the red die.
A die face showing a skull symbol. Required by certain tasks. Peril appears on one face of each green die, one face of the yellow die, and one face of the red die.

A die face showing a tentacle symbol. Required by certain tasks and triggers terror effects when rolled on a failed attempt. Appears on one face of each green die. The yellow die replaces terror with 4-investigation. The red die replaces 1-investigation with wildcard.
A die face unique to the red die that can be used as any single result: 1-investigation, lore, peril, or terror. Extremely flexible and valuable.
The six standard investigation dice. Each has six faces: 1-investigation, 2-investigation, 3-investigation, lore, peril, and terror. All six are rolled at the start of each adventure attempt.

A bonus die typically added by spending a Common Item. Identical to a green die except the terror face is replaced with 4-investigation, making it strictly better. Only one yellow die exists in the game.

A bonus die typically added by spending a Unique Item. Identical to the yellow die except the 1-investigation face is replaced with a wildcard that counts as any result. The most powerful die in the game.

A die placed on a locked die icon on an Adventure card, Mythos card, or Monster marker. A locked die cannot be rolled or used to complete tasks until the card or marker is resolved or removed from play.

Blue tokens representing an investigator's mental health. Each investigator has a maximum sanity shown on their card. Sanity is lost through penalties, terror effects, and certain Mythos cards. If sanity reaches 0, the investigator is devoured.

Red tokens representing an investigator's physical health. Each investigator has a maximum stamina shown on their card. Stamina is lost through penalties, failed assists, and certain game effects. If stamina reaches 0, the investigator is devoured.

Tokens that can be spent after any dice roll to re-roll any or all dice in the pool. Multiple clues can be spent on the same roll. Spent clues return to the shared pool. Some investigators start with clue tokens, and they can be earned as adventure rewards.

The most important tokens in the game. Earned as rewards from certain adventure cards. Placed next to the Ancient One card when collected. When the total equals or exceeds the Ancient One's Elder Sign limit, the investigators win.

Tokens placed on the Ancient One's doom track throughout the game. Added by Mythos card effects, terror penalties, devouring, and other game events. When the doom track is completely filled, the Ancient One awakens and the final battle begins.

A small cardboard token matching each investigator card. Placed on adventure cards or the Entrance to show the investigator's current location. Can also hold one focused or assisted die during an adventure attempt.

A resolved adventure card or defeated monster marker kept by the investigator. Each has a trophy value (usually 2 for adventures, 1 for monsters). Trophies can be spent at the Entrance for items, healing, or other benefits.

Oversized cards representing locations and events in the museum. Each shows one or more tasks (rows of symbols), rewards for success (green area), and penalties for failure (red area). Six are face up at all times, replaced when resolved.

Special adventure cards representing portals to other dimensions. Only enter play when a gate reward is earned. Placed below the normal six adventures. Harder but more rewarding than standard adventures. Not replaced when resolved.

An oversized card depicting the game's main antagonist. Shows the doom track, Elder Sign limit, special ability, attack (used during final battle), and combat task. One Ancient One is chosen per game.

A card representing one of 16 playable investigators. Shows the investigator's name, maximum sanity and stamina, special ability, and starting items. Each player selects or is dealt one investigator at the start of the game.

Small cards representing useful equipment. Most allow spending the item to add the yellow die to your dice pool. Earned as adventure rewards or purchased at the Entrance. Spent items go face down to the bottom of the Common Item deck.

Small cards representing rare artifacts and relics. Most allow spending the item to add the red die to your dice pool. Earned as adventure rewards or purchased at the Entrance. More powerful than Common Items.

Small cards representing arcane knowledge. Most allow placing the card next to the play area before a roll, then saving one die result on the card after rolling. The saved die is available for future rolls until used. Returned to the deck after the die is used.
Small cards representing helpful people who assist the investigators. Each ally has a unique ability described on the card. There are 8 ally cards in the game. Earned as adventure rewards.

Small cards drawn each time the clock strikes midnight. Each has an immediate effect (top half) that fires upon drawing, and a lingering effect (bottom half) that persists until the next Mythos card is drawn. Common immediate effects include adding doom tokens and spawning monsters.

Small square tokens drawn from the monster cup when a monster appears. Placed on monster tasks on adventure cards, adding or replacing task requirements. When completed, the marker is taken as a trophy and its back-side reward is gained.

Special monster markers with a mask symbol, used only when Nyarlathotep is the Ancient One. Added to the monster cup during setup. Function like normal monster markers but are exclusive to Nyarlathotep games.

An opaque container (box lid or bag) holding all monster markers face down. Monsters are drawn randomly from the cup when a game effect says 'a monster appears.' If Nyarlathotep is the Ancient One, mask monsters are added to the cup.

A section of an adventure card bordered in white where monster markers are placed. Can be empty (no requirements until a monster is placed), total (all requirements within the border, fully replaced by the monster), or partial (some requirements inside, some outside the border, combining with the monster).

A horizontal row of symbols on an adventure card representing a set of die result requirements that must be satisfied with a single roll. All tasks on a card must be completed to resolve the adventure. Only one task can be completed per roll.

After failing to complete a task, the active player may place one die (in addition to the mandatory set-aside die) on their own investigator marker. The die's result is preserved and can be used to satisfy a task requirement on future rolls during the same adventure attempt. Each marker holds at most one die.

Similar to focusing, but the die is placed on another investigator's marker who is on the same adventure card. If the active player ultimately fails the adventure, each assisting investigator must lose 1 sanity or 1 stamina.

When an investigator's sanity or stamina is reduced to 0 or below, they are devoured. Add 1 doom token to the doom track. Return the investigator card, marker, items, allies, trophies, and clue tokens to the box and decks. The player receives a new, unused investigator on their next turn.

A penalty printed on some adventure cards and Mythos cards that triggers when a player fails to complete a task and at least one of the rolled dice shows a terror result. Common terror effects include losing sanity, losing stamina, or adding doom tokens.

Game effects that trigger when the clock hand is advanced to XII (midnight). Lingering effects on Mythos cards often specify 'At Midnight' penalties. All At Midnight effects in play resolve before a new Mythos card is drawn.

A row of spaces on the Ancient One card where doom tokens are placed. When the last space is filled, the Ancient One awakens. Some spaces have monster icons, which spawn a monster when a doom token is placed on that space. Doom tokens fill from left to right, top row first.

The number printed on the Ancient One card indicating how many elder sign tokens the investigators need to collect to seal the Ancient One and win the game. Ranges from about 8 to 14 depending on the Ancient One.

The cardboard clock in the center of the table. Its hand advances 3 hours clockwise at the end of each player's turn. It only rests on XII, III, VI, or IX. When it reaches XII (midnight), At Midnight effects resolve and a new Mythos card is drawn.

The reference sheet where investigators start the game and return after adventures are resolved. Instead of attempting an adventure, a player may move to the Entrance and use one of three services: Receive First Aid, Search the Lost & Found, or Buy a Souvenir.
An Entrance activity. Options: regain 1 sanity and 1 stamina for free, or pay 2 trophies to fully restore sanity or stamina, or pay 4 trophies to fully restore both.
An Entrance activity. Roll a green die and consult the chart on the Entrance reference sheet for a random item or effect based on the result.
An Entrance activity. Spend trophies to purchase one item from the Entrance reference sheet's shop list, such as Common Items, Unique Items, Spells, or other benefits.

A dimension beyond our reality, accessed when a gate reward is earned. Represented by Other World adventure cards placed below the normal six adventures. Harder and more rewarding than museum adventures. Not replaced when resolved.

A vast, powerful creature from beyond space and time that the investigators are trying to prevent from awakening. Examples include Yig, Azathoth, Cthulhu, Hastur, and Nyarlathotep. Each has unique abilities and difficulty levels.

An ancient protective symbol discovered by early cultures to ward off the Ancient Ones. In game terms, elder sign tokens are the primary win condition. Collecting enough of them seals the Ancient One away before it can awaken.
Father of Serpents. One of the eight Ancient Ones. Generally considered the easiest Ancient One for beginners, with a smaller doom track and straightforward mechanics.

The Blind Idiot God. One of the eight Ancient Ones. Unique in that there is no final battle — if the doom track fills, the investigators immediately lose the game. This makes Azathoth games a pure race against doom.
The Dreamer in the Deep. One of the eight Ancient Ones. Has a larger doom track but devastating attacks during the final battle. A classic Lovecraftian horror.
The King in Yellow. One of the eight Ancient Ones. Introduces unique mechanics that can disrupt standard strategies.

The Crawling Chaos. One of the eight Ancient Ones. The only Ancient One that uses mask monster markers, adding them to the monster cup during setup. Creates additional challenges through these special monsters.

A brave individual who explores the museum and confronts the supernatural. Each of the 16 investigators has unique maximum sanity, maximum stamina, a special ability, and starting items. Players control one investigator at a time.
A portal to another dimension. Earned as a reward on some adventure cards. When gained, draw an Other World adventure card and add it to play below the normal six adventures.

A task symbol showing two different results separated by a line. Either of the two shown results will satisfy this requirement. For example, a split showing lore/peril means rolling either lore or peril will complete that part of the task.
